However, in the first year of {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|貞享}} (1684), {{ill|Shibukawa Shunkai|ja|渋川春海}} created the {{ill|Jōkyō calendar|ja|貞享暦}}, replacing the traditional {{ill|Senmyō calendar|ja|宣明暦}}. Following this achievement, the Shogunate established the Tenmonkata under the supervision of the {{ill|Jisha Bugyō|ja|寺社奉行}}. On {{ill|December 1, 1684|ja|貞享元年12月1日}} (January 5, 1685), Shunkai was appointed the first Tenmonkata, which also became the name of the role. Calendar-making responsibilities shifted from the Imperial Court to the Shogunate, and the Tenmonkata carried out its tasks under Shogunate authority. Initially under the Jisha Bugyō, it was placed under the {{ill|Wakadoshiyori|ja|若年寄}} on {{ill|January 23, 1747|ja|延享4年1月23日}}.
The position was hereditary, but capable individuals with expertise in astronomy were occasionally appointed through adoption or special selection. By the end of the Edo period, eight families held the position: the {{ill|Shibukawa family|ja|渋川家}}, {{ill|Inokai family|ja|猪飼家}}, {{ill|Nishikawa family|ja|西川家}}, {{ill|Yamaji family|ja|山路家}}, {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}}, {{ill|Okumura family|ja|奥村家}}, {{ill|Takahashi family|ja|高橋家}}, and {{ill|Adachi family|ja|足立家}}. Some families, such as those of {{ill|Nishikawa Joken|ja|西川如見}} and {{ill|Takahashi Yoshitoki|ja|高橋至時}}, did not maintain their roles until the end of the period. By the late Edo period, only the Shibukawa, Yamaji, and Adachi families continued in the position.
In the 8th year of {{ill|Bunka|ja|文化}} (1811), {{ill|Takahashi Kageyasu|ja|高橋景保}} proposed establishing the {{ill|Bansho Wage Goyo|ja|蛮書和解御用}}, which lasted until the establishment of the {{ill|Institute for Western Studies|ja|蕃書調所}} in {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1858). By the late Edo period, the Tenmonkata also handled astronomical observations, surveying, geography, and the translation of Western books, serving as one of the predecessors of {{ill|The University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== The Tenmonkata Families ==
; Shibukawa Family : Owing to Shibukawa Shunkai's achievements, his family assumed the role of Tenmonkata in Jōkyō 1 (1684) and maintained the position, despite several adoptions, until the late Edo period. : {{ill|Shibukawa Shunkai|ja|渋川春海}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mukai|ja|渋川昔尹}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiin|ja|渋川敬尹}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiya|ja|渋川敬也}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Noriyasu|ja|渋川則休}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mitsuhiro|ja|渋川光洪}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masakiyo|ja|渋川正清}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masayō|ja|渋川正陽}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiyū|ja|渋川景佑}} – ({{ill|Shibukawa Keichoku|ja|渋川敬直}}) – {{ill|Shibukawa Sukemasa|ja|渋川佑賢}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiten|ja|渋川敬典}}
; Inokai Family : {{ill|Inokai Masakazu|ja|猪飼正一}} became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} 1 (1716). After his death in {{ill|Kanpō|ja|寛保}} 1 (1741), the family ceased its role.
; Nishikawa Family : Nishikawa Joken’s son, {{ill|Nishikawa Masayasu|ja|西川正休}}, was appointed in {{ill|Enkyō|ja|延享}} 4 (1747). The position lasted for two generations. : {{ill|Nishikawa Masayasu|ja|西川正休}} – {{ill|Nishikawa Tadataka|ja|西川忠喬}}
; Yamaji Family : {{ill|Yamaji Sukezumi|ja|山路主住}} became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Meiwa|ja|明和}} 1 (1764). His descendants continued until the end of the Edo period. : {{ill|Yamaji Sukezumi|ja|山路主住}} – ({{ill|Yamaji Yukiyuki|ja|山路之徽}}) – {{ill|Yamaji Tokufū|ja|山路徳風}} – {{ill|Yamaji Kanetaka|ja|山路諧孝}} – {{ill|Yamaji Akitsune|ja|山路彰常}} – ({{ill|Yamaji Akizumi|ja|山路彰善}})
; Yoshida Family : {{ill|Sasaki Nagahide|ja|佐々木長秀}}, later {{ill|Yoshida Nagahide|ja|吉田秀長}}, became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Meiwa|ja|明和}} 1 (1764). : {{ill|Yoshida Nagahide|ja|吉田秀長}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀升}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀賢}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀茂}}
== Observatories == The first observatory, '''Sitendai''', was established in {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|貞享}} 2 (1685) in {{ill|Ushigome Wakamachi|ja|牛込藁町}}. It moved to {{ill|Kanda Surugadai|ja|神田駿河台}} in {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 14 (1701) and later to {{ill|Asakusa|ja|浅草}}. In {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 13 (1842), a second observatory was established in {{ill|Kudanzaka|ja|九段坂}}. Both were abolished in {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 2 (1869).
== Abolition of the Tenmonkata == After the {{ill|Taisei Hōkan|ja|大政奉還}}, responsibility for calendrical work returned to the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}}. In {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 3 (1870), calendrical duties were transferred to the Ministry of Education's {{ill|Astronomical Bureau|ja|天文暦道局}}.
== Related Articles == {{div col}} * {{ill|Inō Tadataka|ja|伊能忠敬}} * {{ill|Mitsukuri Shūkichi|ja|箕作秋坪}} * {{ill|National Astronomical Observatory of Japan|ja|国立天文台}} {{div col end}}

{{Citation needed|date=July 2020}} '''Tenmonkata''' (天文方) was the astronomical and calendrical research institution established by the [[Tokugawa Shogunate]]. Its primary responsibility was overseeing the creation of calendars ({{ill|Henreki|ja|編暦}}).
== Overview ==
Initially, calendar creation was the responsibility of the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, a bureau under the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷}} and supervised by the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}}. However, in the first year of {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|貞享}} (1684), {{ill|Shibukawa Shunkai|ja|渋川春海}} created the {{ill|Jōkyō calendar|ja|貞享暦}}, replacing the traditional {{ill|Senmyō calendar|ja|宣明暦}}. Following this achievement, the Shogunate established the Tenmonkata under the supervision of the {{ill|Jisha Bugyō|ja|寺社奉行}}. On {{ill|December 1, 1684|ja|貞享元年12月1日}} (January 5, 1685), Shunkai was appointed the first Tenmonkata, which also became the name of the role. Calendar-making responsibilities shifted from the Imperial Court to the Shogunate, and the Tenmonkata carried out its tasks under Shogunate authority. Initially under the Jisha Bugyō, it was placed under the {{ill|Wakadoshiyori|ja|若年寄}} on {{ill|January 23, 1747|ja|延享4年1月23日}}.
The position was hereditary, but capable individuals with expertise in astronomy were occasionally appointed through adoption or special selection. By the end of the Edo period, eight families held the position: the {{ill|Shibukawa family|ja|渋川家}}, {{ill|Inokai family|ja|猪飼家}}, {{ill|Nishikawa family|ja|西川家}}, {{ill|Yamaji family|ja|山路家}}, {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}}, {{ill|Okumura family|ja|奥村家}}, {{ill|Takahashi family|ja|高橋家}}, and {{ill|Adachi family|ja|足立家}}. Some families, such as those of {{ill|Nishikawa Joken|ja|西川如見}} and {{ill|Takahashi Yoshitoki|ja|高橋至時}}, did not maintain their roles until the end of the period. By the late Edo period, only the Shibukawa, Yamaji, and Adachi families continued in the position.
In the 8th year of {{ill|Bunka|ja|文化}} (1811), {{ill|Takahashi Kageyasu|ja|高橋景保}} proposed establishing the {{ill|Bansho Wage Goyo|ja|蛮書和解御用}}, which lasted until the establishment of the {{ill|Institute for Western Studies|ja|蕃書調所}} in {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1858). By the late Edo period, the Tenmonkata also handled astronomical observations, surveying, geography, and the translation of Western books, serving as one of the predecessors of {{ill|The University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== The Tenmonkata Families ==
; Shibukawa Family : Owing to Shibukawa Shunkai's achievements, his family assumed the role of Tenmonkata in Jōkyō 1 (1684) and maintained the position, despite several adoptions, until the late Edo period. : {{ill|Shibukawa Shunkai|ja|渋川春海}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mukai|ja|渋川昔尹}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiin|ja|渋川敬尹}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiya|ja|渋川敬也}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Noriyasu|ja|渋川則休}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mitsuhiro|ja|渋川光洪}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masakiyo|ja|渋川正清}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masayō|ja|渋川正陽}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiyū|ja|渋川景佑}} – ({{ill|Shibukawa Keichoku|ja|渋川敬直}}) – {{ill|Shibukawa Sukemasa|ja|渋川佑賢}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiten|ja|渋川敬典}}
; Inokai Family : {{ill|Inokai Masakazu|ja|猪飼正一}} became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} 1 (1716). After his death in {{ill|Kanpō|ja|寛保}} 1 (1741), the family ceased its role.
; Nishikawa Family : Nishikawa Joken’s son, {{ill|Nishikawa Masayasu|ja|西川正休}}, was appointed in {{ill|Enkyō|ja|延享}} 4 (1747). The position lasted for two generations. : {{ill|Nishikawa Masayasu|ja|西川正休}} – {{ill|Nishikawa Tadataka|ja|西川忠喬}}
; Yamaji Family : {{ill|Yamaji Sukezumi|ja|山路主住}} became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Meiwa|ja|明和}} 1 (1764). His descendants continued until the end of the Edo period. : {{ill|Yamaji Sukezumi|ja|山路主住}} – ({{ill|Yamaji Yukiyuki|ja|山路之徽}}) – {{ill|Yamaji Tokufū|ja|山路徳風}} – {{ill|Yamaji Kanetaka|ja|山路諧孝}} – {{ill|Yamaji Akitsune|ja|山路彰常}} – ({{ill|Yamaji Akizumi|ja|山路彰善}})
; Yoshida Family : {{ill|Sasaki Nagahide|ja|佐々木長秀}}, later {{ill|Yoshida Nagahide|ja|吉田秀長}}, became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Meiwa|ja|明和}} 1 (1764). : {{ill|Yoshida Nagahide|ja|吉田秀長}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀升}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀賢}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀茂}}
== Observatories == The first observatory, '''Sitendai''', was established in {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|貞享}} 2 (1685) in {{ill|Ushigome Wakamachi|ja|牛込藁町}}. It moved to {{ill|Kanda Surugadai|ja|神田駿河台}} in {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 14 (1701) and later to {{ill|Asakusa|ja|浅草}}. In {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 13 (1842), a second observatory was established in {{ill|Kudanzaka|ja|九段坂}}. Both were abolished in {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 2 (1869).
== Abolition of the Tenmonkata == After the {{ill|Taisei Hōkan|ja|大政奉還}}, responsibility for calendrical work returned to the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}}. In {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 3 (1870), calendrical duties were transferred to the Ministry of Education's {{ill|Astronomical Bureau|ja|天文暦道局}}.
== Related Articles == {{div col}} * {{ill|Inō Tadataka|ja|伊能忠敬}} * {{ill|Mitsukuri Shūkichi|ja|箕作秋坪}} * {{ill|National Astronomical Observatory of Japan|ja|国立天文台}} {{div col end}}

{{Citation needed|date=July 2020}} '''Tenmonkata''' (天文方) was the astronomical and calendrical research institution established by the [[Tokugawa Shogunate]]. Its primary responsibility was overseeing the creation of calendars ({{ill|Henreki|ja|編暦}}).
== Overview ==
Initially, calendar creation was the responsibility of the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, a bureau under the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷}} and supervised by the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}}. However, in the first year of {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|貞享}} (1684), {{ill|Shibukawa Shunkai|ja|渋川春海}} created the {{ill|Jōkyō calendar|ja|貞享暦}}, replacing the traditional {{ill|Senmyō calendar|ja|宣明暦}}. Following this achievement, the Shogunate established the Tenmonkata under the supervision of the {{ill|Jisha Bugyō|ja|寺社奉行}}. On {{ill|December 1, 1684|ja|貞享元年12月1日}} (January 5, 1685), Shunkai was appointed the first Tenmonkata, which also became the name of the role. Calendar-making responsibilities shifted from the Imperial Court to the Shogunate, and the Tenmonkata carried out its tasks under Shogunate authority. Initially under the Jisha Bugyō, it was placed under the {{ill|Wakadoshiyori|ja|若年寄}} on {{ill|January 23, 1747|ja|延享4年1月23日}}.
The position was hereditary, but capable individuals with expertise in astronomy were occasionally appointed through adoption or special selection. By the end of the Edo period, eight families held the position: the {{ill|Shibukawa family|ja|渋川家}}, {{ill|Inokai family|ja|猪飼家}}, {{ill|Nishikawa family|ja|西川家}}, {{ill|Yamaji family|ja|山路家}}, {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}}, {{ill|Okumura family|ja|奥村家}}, {{ill|Takahashi family|ja|高橋家}}, and {{ill|Adachi family|ja|足立家}}. Some families, such as those of {{ill|Nishikawa Joken|ja|西川如見}} and {{ill|Takahashi Yoshitoki|ja|高橋至時}}, did not maintain their roles until the end of the period. By the late Edo period, only the Shibukawa, Yamaji, and Adachi families continued in the position.
In the 8th year of {{ill|Bunka|ja|文化}} (1811), {{ill|Takahashi Kageyasu|ja|高橋景保}} proposed establishing the {{ill|Bansho Wage Goyo|ja|蛮書和解御用}}, which lasted until the establishment of the {{ill|Institute for Western Studies|ja|蕃書調所}} in {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1858). By the late Edo period, the Tenmonkata also handled astronomical observations, surveying, geography, and the translation of Western books, serving as one of the predecessors of {{ill|The University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== The Tenmonkata Families ==
; Shibukawa Family : Owing to Shibukawa Shunkai's achievements, his family assumed the role of Tenmonkata in Jōkyō 1 (1684) and maintained the position, despite several adoptions, until the late Edo period. : {{ill|Shibukawa Shunkai|ja|渋川春海}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mukai|ja|渋川昔尹}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiin|ja|渋川敬尹}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiya|ja|渋川敬也}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Noriyasu|ja|渋川則休}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mitsuhiro|ja|渋川光洪}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masakiyo|ja|渋川正清}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masayō|ja|渋川正陽}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiyū|ja|渋川景佑}} – ({{ill|Shibukawa Keichoku|ja|渋川敬直}}) – {{ill|Shibukawa Sukemasa|ja|渋川佑賢}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiten|ja|渋川敬典}}
; Inokai Family : {{ill|Inokai Masakazu|ja|猪飼正一}} became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} 1 (1716). After his death in {{ill|Kanpō|ja|寛保}} 1 (1741), the family ceased its role.
; Nishikawa Family : Nishikawa Joken’s son, {{ill|Nishikawa Masayasu|ja|西川正休}}, was appointed in {{ill|Enkyō|ja|延享}} 4 (1747). The position lasted for two generations. : {{ill|Nishikawa Masayasu|ja|西川正休}} – {{ill|Nishikawa Tadataka|ja|西川忠喬}}
; Yamaji Family : {{ill|Yamaji Sukezumi|ja|山路主住}} became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Meiwa|ja|明和}} 1 (1764). His descendants continued until the end of the Edo period. : {{ill|Yamaji Sukezumi|ja|山路主住}} – ({{ill|Yamaji Yukiyuki|ja|山路之徽}}) – {{ill|Yamaji Tokufū|ja|山路徳風}} – {{ill|Yamaji Kanetaka|ja|山路諧孝}} – {{ill|Yamaji Akitsune|ja|山路彰常}} – ({{ill|Yamaji Akizumi|ja|山路彰善}})
; Yoshida Family : {{ill|Sasaki Nagahide|ja|佐々木長秀}}, later {{ill|Yoshida Nagahide|ja|吉田秀長}}, became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Meiwa|ja|明和}} 1 (1764). : {{ill|Yoshida Nagahide|ja|吉田秀長}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀升}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀賢}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀茂}}
== Observatories == The first observatory, '''Sitendai''', was established in {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|貞享}} 2 (1685) in {{ill|Ushigome Wakamachi|ja|牛込藁町}}. It moved to {{ill|Kanda Surugadai|ja|神田駿河台}} in {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 14 (1701) and later to {{ill|Asakusa|ja|浅草}}. In {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 13 (1842), a second observatory was established in {{ill|Kudanzaka|ja|九段坂}}. Both were abolished in {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 2 (1869).
== Abolition of the Tenmonkata == After the {{ill|Taisei Hōkan|ja|大政奉還}}, responsibility for calendrical work returned to the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}}. In {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 3 (1870), calendrical duties were transferred to the Ministry of Education's {{ill|Astronomical Bureau|ja|天文暦道局}}.
== Related Articles == {{div col}} * {{ill|Inō Tadataka|ja|伊能忠敬}} * {{ill|Mitsukuri Shūkichi|ja|箕作秋坪}} * {{ill|National Astronomical Observatory of Japan|ja|国立天文台}} {{div col end}}

; Shibukawa Family : Owing to Shibukawa Shunkai's achievements, his family assumed the role of Tenmonkata in Jōkyō 1 (1684) and maintained the position, despite several adoptions, until the late Edo period. : {{ill|Shibukawa Shunkai|ja|渋川春海}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mukai|ja|渋川昔尹}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiin|ja|渋川敬尹}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiya|ja|渋川敬也}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Noriyasu|ja|渋川則休}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mitsuhiro|ja|渋川光洪}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masakiyo|ja|渋川正清}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masayō|ja|渋川正陽}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiyū|ja|渋川景佑}} – ({{ill|Shibukawa Keichoku|ja|渋川敬直}}) – {{ill|Shibukawa Sukemasa|ja|渋川佑賢}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiten|ja|渋川敬典}}
; Inokai Family : {{ill|Inokai Masakazu|ja|猪飼正一}} became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} 1 (1716). After his death in {{ill|Kanpō|ja|寛保}} 1 (1741), the family ceased its role.
; Nishikawa Family : Nishikawa Joken’s son, {{ill|Nishikawa Masayasu|ja|西川正休}}, was appointed in {{ill|Enkyō|ja|延享}} 4 (1747). The position lasted for two generations. : {{ill|Nishikawa Masayasu|ja|西川正休}} – {{ill|Nishikawa Tadataka|ja|西川忠喬}}
; Yamaji Family : {{ill|Yamaji Sukezumi|ja|山路主住}} became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Meiwa|ja|明和}} 1 (1764). His descendants continued until the end of the Edo period. : {{ill|Yamaji Sukezumi|ja|山路主住}} – ({{ill|Yamaji Yukiyuki|ja|山路之徽}}) – {{ill|Yamaji Tokufū|ja|山路徳風}} – {{ill|Yamaji Kanetaka|ja|山路諧孝}} – {{ill|Yamaji Akitsune|ja|山路彰常}} – ({{ill|Yamaji Akizumi|ja|山路彰善}})
; Yoshida Family : {{ill|Sasaki Nagahide|ja|佐々木長秀}}, later {{ill|Yoshida Nagahide|ja|吉田秀長}}, became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Meiwa|ja|明和}} 1 (1764). : {{ill|Yoshida Nagahide|ja|吉田秀長}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀升}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀賢}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀茂}}
== Observatories == The first observatory, '''Sitendai''', was established in {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|貞享}} 2 (1685) in {{ill|Ushigome Wakamachi|ja|牛込藁町}}. It moved to {{ill|Kanda Surugadai|ja|神田駿河台}} in {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 14 (1701) and later to {{ill|Asakusa|ja|浅草}}. In {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 13 (1842), a second observatory was established in {{ill|Kudanzaka|ja|九段坂}}. Both were abolished in {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 2 (1869).
== Abolition of the Tenmonkata == After the {{ill|Taisei Hōkan|ja|大政奉還}}, responsibility for calendrical work returned to the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}}. In {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 3 (1870), calendrical duties were transferred to the Ministry of Education's {{ill|Astronomical Bureau|ja|天文暦道局}}.
== Related Articles == {{div col}} * {{ill|Inō Tadataka|ja|伊能忠敬}} * {{ill|Mitsukuri Shūkichi|ja|箕作秋坪}} * {{ill|National Astronomical Observatory of Japan|ja|国立天文台}} {{div col end}}

revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=July 2020}} '''Tenmonkata''' (天文方) was the astronomical and calendrical research institution established by the [[Tokugawa Shogunate]]. Its primary responsibility was overseeing the creation of calendars ({{ill|Henreki|ja|編暦}}).
== Overview ==
Initially, calendar creation was the responsibility of the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, a bureau under the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷}} and supervised by the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}}. However, in the first year of {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|貞享}} (1684), {{ill|Shibukawa Shunkai|ja|渋川春海}} created the {{ill|Jōkyō calendar|ja|貞享暦}}, replacing the traditional {{ill|Senmyō calendar|ja|宣明暦}}. Following this achievement, the Shogunate established the Tenmonkata under the supervision of the {{ill|Jisha Bugyō|ja|寺社奉行}}. On {{ill|December 1, 1684|ja|貞享元年12月1日}} (January 5, 1685), Shunkai was appointed the first Tenmonkata, which also became the name of the role. Calendar-making responsibilities shifted from the Imperial Court to the Shogunate, and the Tenmonkata carried out its tasks under Shogunate authority. Initially under the Jisha Bugyō, it was placed under the {{ill|Wakadoshiyori|ja|若年寄}} on {{ill|January 23, 1747|ja|延享4年1月23日}}.
The position was hereditary, but capable individuals with expertise in astronomy were occasionally appointed through adoption or special selection. By the end of the Edo period, eight families held the position: the {{ill|Shibukawa family|ja|渋川家}}, {{ill|Inokai family|ja|猪飼家}}, {{ill|Nishikawa family|ja|西川家}}, {{ill|Yamaji family|ja|山路家}}, {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}}, {{ill|Okumura family|ja|奥村家}}, {{ill|Takahashi family|ja|高橋家}}, and {{ill|Adachi family|ja|足立家}}. Some families, such as those of {{ill|Nishikawa Joken|ja|西川如見}} and {{ill|Takahashi Yoshitoki|ja|高橋至時}}, did not maintain their roles until the end of the period. By the late Edo period, only the Shibukawa, Yamaji, and Adachi families continued in the position.
In the 8th year of {{ill|Bunka|ja|文化}} (1811), {{ill|Takahashi Kageyasu|ja|高橋景保}} proposed establishing the {{ill|Bansho Wage Goyo|ja|蛮書和解御用}}, which lasted until the establishment of the {{ill|Institute for Western Studies|ja|蕃書調所}} in {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1858). By the late Edo period, the Tenmonkata also handled astronomical observations, surveying, geography, and the translation of Western books, serving as one of the predecessors of {{ill|The University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== The Tenmonkata Families ==
; Shibukawa Family : Owing to Shibukawa Shunkai's achievements, his family assumed the role of Tenmonkata in Jōkyō 1 (1684) and maintained the position, despite several adoptions, until the late Edo period. : {{ill|Shibukawa Shunkai|ja|渋川春海}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mukai|ja|渋川昔尹}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiin|ja|渋川敬尹}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiya|ja|渋川敬也}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Noriyasu|ja|渋川則休}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mitsuhiro|ja|渋川光洪}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masakiyo|ja|渋川正清}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masayō|ja|渋川正陽}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiyū|ja|渋川景佑}} – ({{ill|Shibukawa Keichoku|ja|渋川敬直}}) – {{ill|Shibukawa Sukemasa|ja|渋川佑賢}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiten|ja|渋川敬典}}
; Inokai Family : {{ill|Inokai Masakazu|ja|猪飼正一}} became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} 1 (1716). After his death in {{ill|Kanpō|ja|寛保}} 1 (1741), the family ceased its role.
; Nishikawa Family : Nishikawa Joken’s son, {{ill|Nishikawa Masayasu|ja|西川正休}}, was appointed in {{ill|Enkyō|ja|延享}} 4 (1747). The position lasted for two generations. : {{ill|Nishikawa Masayasu|ja|西川正休}} – {{ill|Nishikawa Tadataka|ja|西川忠喬}}
; Yamaji Family : {{ill|Yamaji Sukezumi|ja|山路主住}} became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Meiwa|ja|明和}} 1 (1764). His descendants continued until the end of the Edo period. : {{ill|Yamaji Sukezumi|ja|山路主住}} – ({{ill|Yamaji Yukiyuki|ja|山路之徽}}) – {{ill|Yamaji Tokufū|ja|山路徳風}} – {{ill|Yamaji Kanetaka|ja|山路諧孝}} – {{ill|Yamaji Akitsune|ja|山路彰常}} – ({{ill|Yamaji Akizumi|ja|山路彰善}})
; Yoshida Family : {{ill|Sasaki Nagahide|ja|佐々木長秀}}, later {{ill|Yoshida Nagahide|ja|吉田秀長}}, became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Meiwa|ja|明和}} 1 (1764). : {{ill|Yoshida Nagahide|ja|吉田秀長}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀升}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀賢}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀茂}}
== Observatories == The first observatory, '''Sitendai''', was established in {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|貞享}} 2 (1685) in {{ill|Ushigome Wakamachi|ja|牛込藁町}}. It moved to {{ill|Kanda Surugadai|ja|神田駿河台}} in {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 14 (1701) and later to {{ill|Asakusa|ja|浅草}}. In {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 13 (1842), a second observatory was established in {{ill|Kudanzaka|ja|九段坂}}. Both were abolished in {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 2 (1869).
== Abolition of the Tenmonkata == After the {{ill|Taisei Hōkan|ja|大政奉還}}, responsibility for calendrical work returned to the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}}. In {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 3 (1870), calendrical duties were transferred to the Ministry of Education's {{ill|Astronomical Bureau|ja|天文暦道局}}.
== Related Articles == {{div col}} * {{ill|Inō Tadataka|ja|伊能忠敬}} * {{ill|Mitsukuri Shūkichi|ja|箕作秋坪}} * {{ill|National Astronomical Observatory of Japan|ja|国立天文台}} {{div col end}}

{{Citation needed|date=July 2020}} '''Tenmonkata''' (天文方) was the astronomical and calendrical research institution established by the [[Tokugawa Shogunate]]. Its primary responsibility was overseeing the creation of calendars ({{ill|Henreki|ja|編暦}}).
== Overview ==
Initially, calendar creation was the responsibility of the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, a bureau under the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷}} and supervised by the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}}. However, in the first year of {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|貞享}} (1684), {{ill|Shibukawa Shunkai|ja|渋川春海}} created the {{ill|Jōkyō calendar|ja|貞享暦}}, replacing the traditional {{ill|Senmyō calendar|ja|宣明暦}}. Following this achievement, the Shogunate established the Tenmonkata under the supervision of the {{ill|Jisha Bugyō|ja|寺社奉行}}. On {{ill|December 1, 1684|ja|貞享元年12月1日}} (January 5, 1685), Shunkai was appointed the first Tenmonkata, which also became the name of the role. Calendar-making responsibilities shifted from the Imperial Court to the Shogunate, and the Tenmonkata carried out its tasks under Shogunate authority. Initially under the Jisha Bugyō, it was placed under the {{ill|Wakadoshiyori|ja|若年寄}} on {{ill|January 23, 1747|ja|延享4年1月23日}}.
The position was hereditary, but capable individuals with expertise in astronomy were occasionally appointed through adoption or special selection. By the end of the Edo period, eight families held the position: the {{ill|Shibukawa family|ja|渋川家}}, {{ill|Inokai family|ja|猪飼家}}, {{ill|Nishikawa family|ja|西川家}}, {{ill|Yamaji family|ja|山路家}}, {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}}, {{ill|Okumura family|ja|奥村家}}, {{ill|Takahashi family|ja|高橋家}}, and {{ill|Adachi family|ja|足立家}}. Some families, such as those of {{ill|Nishikawa Joken|ja|西川如見}} and {{ill|Takahashi Yoshitoki|ja|高橋至時}}, did not maintain their roles until the end of the period. By the late Edo period, only the Shibukawa, Yamaji, and Adachi families continued in the position.
In the 8th year of {{ill|Bunka|ja|文化}} (1811), {{ill|Takahashi Kageyasu|ja|高橋景保}} proposed establishing the {{ill|Bansho Wage Goyo|ja|蛮書和解御用}}, which lasted until the establishment of the {{ill|Institute for Western Studies|ja|蕃書調所}} in {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1858). By the late Edo period, the Tenmonkata also handled astronomical observations, surveying, geography, and the translation of Western books, serving as one of the predecessors of {{ill|The University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== The Tenmonkata Families ==
; Shibukawa Family : Owing to Shibukawa Shunkai's achievements, his family assumed the role of Tenmonkata in Jōkyō 1 (1684) and maintained the position, despite several adoptions, until the late Edo period. : {{ill|Shibukawa Shunkai|ja|渋川春海}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mukai|ja|渋川昔尹}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiin|ja|渋川敬尹}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiya|ja|渋川敬也}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Noriyasu|ja|渋川則休}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mitsuhiro|ja|渋川光洪}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masakiyo|ja|渋川正清}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masayō|ja|渋川正陽}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiyū|ja|渋川景佑}} – ({{ill|Shibukawa Keichoku|ja|渋川敬直}}) – {{ill|Shibukawa Sukemasa|ja|渋川佑賢}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiten|ja|渋川敬典}}
; Inokai Family : {{ill|Inokai Masakazu|ja|猪飼正一}} became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} 1 (1716). After his death in {{ill|Kanpō|ja|寛保}} 1 (1741), the family ceased its role.
; Nishikawa Family : Nishikawa Joken’s son, {{ill|Nishikawa Masayasu|ja|西川正休}}, was appointed in {{ill|Enkyō|ja|延享}} 4 (1747). The position lasted for two generations. : {{ill|Nishikawa Masayasu|ja|西川正休}} – {{ill|Nishikawa Tadataka|ja|西川忠喬}}
; Yamaji Family : {{ill|Yamaji Sukezumi|ja|山路主住}} became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Meiwa|ja|明和}} 1 (1764). His descendants continued until the end of the Edo period. : {{ill|Yamaji Sukezumi|ja|山路主住}} – ({{ill|Yamaji Yukiyuki|ja|山路之徽}}) – {{ill|Yamaji Tokufū|ja|山路徳風}} – {{ill|Yamaji Kanetaka|ja|山路諧孝}} – {{ill|Yamaji Akitsune|ja|山路彰常}} – ({{ill|Yamaji Akizumi|ja|山路彰善}})
; Yoshida Family : {{ill|Sasaki Nagahide|ja|佐々木長秀}}, later {{ill|Yoshida Nagahide|ja|吉田秀長}}, became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Meiwa|ja|明和}} 1 (1764). : {{ill|Yoshida Nagahide|ja|吉田秀長}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀升}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀賢}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀茂}}
== Observatories == The first observatory, '''Sitendai''', was established in {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|貞享}} 2 (1685) in {{ill|Ushigome Wakamachi|ja|牛込藁町}}. It moved to {{ill|Kanda Surugadai|ja|神田駿河台}} in {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 14 (1701) and later to {{ill|Asakusa|ja|浅草}}. In {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 13 (1842), a second observatory was established in {{ill|Kudanzaka|ja|九段坂}}. Both were abolished in {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 2 (1869).
== Abolition of the Tenmonkata == After the {{ill|Taisei Hōkan|ja|大政奉還}}, responsibility for calendrical work returned to the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}}. In {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 3 (1870), calendrical duties were transferred to the Ministry of Education's {{ill|Astronomical Bureau|ja|天文暦道局}}.
== Related Articles == {{div col}} * {{ill|Inō Tadataka|ja|伊能忠敬}} * {{ill|Mitsukuri Shūkichi|ja|箕作秋坪}} * {{ill|National Astronomical Observatory of Japan|ja|国立天文台}} {{div col end}}

Some families, such as those of {{ill|Nishikawa Joken|ja|西川如見}} and {{ill|Takahashi Yoshitoki|ja|高橋至時}}, did not maintain their roles until the end of the period. By the late Edo period, only the Shibukawa, Yamaji, and Adachi families continued in the position.
In the 8th year of {{ill|Bunka|ja|文化}} (1811), {{ill|Takahashi Kageyasu|ja|高橋景保}} proposed establishing the {{ill|Bansho Wage Goyo|ja|蛮書和解御用}}, which lasted until the establishment of the {{ill|Institute for Western Studies|ja|蕃書調所}} in {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1858). By the late Edo period, the Tenmonkata also handled astronomical observations, surveying, geography, and the translation of Western books, serving as one of the predecessors of {{ill|The University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== The Tenmonkata Families ==
; Shibukawa Family : Owing to Shibukawa Shunkai's achievements, his family assumed the role of Tenmonkata in Jōkyō 1 (1684) and maintained the position, despite several adoptions, until the late Edo period. : {{ill|Shibukawa Shunkai|ja|渋川春海}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mukai|ja|渋川昔尹}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiin|ja|渋川敬尹}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiya|ja|渋川敬也}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Noriyasu|ja|渋川則休}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mitsuhiro|ja|渋川光洪}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masakiyo|ja|渋川正清}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masayō|ja|渋川正陽}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiyū|ja|渋川景佑}} – ({{ill|Shibukawa Keichoku|ja|渋川敬直}}) – {{ill|Shibukawa Sukemasa|ja|渋川佑賢}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiten|ja|渋川敬典}}
; Inokai Family : {{ill|Inokai Masakazu|ja|猪飼正一}} became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} 1 (1716). After his death in {{ill|Kanpō|ja|寛保}} 1 (1741), the family ceased its role.
; Nishikawa Family : Nishikawa Joken’s son, {{ill|Nishikawa Masayasu|ja|西川正休}}, was appointed in {{ill|Enkyō|ja|延享}} 4 (1747). The position lasted for two generations. : {{ill|Nishikawa Masayasu|ja|西川正休}} – {{ill|Nishikawa Tadataka|ja|西川忠喬}}
; Yamaji Family : {{ill|Yamaji Sukezumi|ja|山路主住}} became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Meiwa|ja|明和}} 1 (1764). His descendants continued until the end of the Edo period. : {{ill|Yamaji Sukezumi|ja|山路主住}} – ({{ill|Yamaji Yukiyuki|ja|山路之徽}}) – {{ill|Yamaji Tokufū|ja|山路徳風}} – {{ill|Yamaji Kanetaka|ja|山路諧孝}} – {{ill|Yamaji Akitsune|ja|山路彰常}} – ({{ill|Yamaji Akizumi|ja|山路彰善}})
; Yoshida Family : {{ill|Sasaki Nagahide|ja|佐々木長秀}}, later {{ill|Yoshida Nagahide|ja|吉田秀長}}, became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Meiwa|ja|明和}} 1 (1764). : {{ill|Yoshida Nagahide|ja|吉田秀長}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀升}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀賢}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀茂}}
== Observatories == The first observatory, '''Sitendai''', was established in {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|貞享}} 2 (1685) in {{ill|Ushigome Wakamachi|ja|牛込藁町}}. It moved to {{ill|Kanda Surugadai|ja|神田駿河台}} in {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 14 (1701) and later to {{ill|Asakusa|ja|浅草}}. In {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 13 (1842), a second observatory was established in {{ill|Kudanzaka|ja|九段坂}}. Both were abolished in {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 2 (1869).

{{Citation needed|date=July 2020}} '''Tenmonkata''' (天文方) was the astronomical and calendrical research institution established by the [[Tokugawa Shogunate]]. Its primary responsibility was overseeing the creation of calendars ({{ill|Henreki|ja|編暦}}).
== Overview ==
Initially, calendar creation was the responsibility of the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, a bureau under the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷}} and supervised by the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}}. However, in the first year of {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|貞享}} (1684), {{ill|Shibukawa Shunkai|ja|渋川春海}} created the {{ill|Jōkyō calendar|ja|貞享暦}}, replacing the traditional {{ill|Senmyō calendar|ja|宣明暦}}. Following this achievement, the Shogunate established the Tenmonkata under the supervision of the {{ill|Jisha Bugyō|ja|寺社奉行}}. On {{ill|December 1, 1684|ja|貞享元年12月1日}} (January 5, 1685), Shunkai was appointed the first Tenmonkata, which also became the name of the role. Calendar-making responsibilities shifted from the Imperial Court to the Shogunate, and the Tenmonkata carried out its tasks under Shogunate authority. Initially under the Jisha Bugyō, it was placed under the {{ill|Wakadoshiyori|ja|若年寄}} on {{ill|January 23, 1747|ja|延享4年1月23日}}.
The position was hereditary, but capable individuals with expertise in astronomy were occasionally appointed through adoption or special selection. By the end of the Edo period, eight families held the position: the {{ill|Shibukawa family|ja|渋川家}}, {{ill|Inokai family|ja|猪飼家}}, {{ill|Nishikawa family|ja|西川家}}, {{ill|Yamaji family|ja|山路家}}, {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}}, {{ill|Okumura family|ja|奥村家}}, {{ill|Takahashi family|ja|高橋家}}, and {{ill|Adachi family|ja|足立家}}. Some families, such as those of {{ill|Nishikawa Joken|ja|西川如見}} and {{ill|Takahashi Yoshitoki|ja|高橋至時}}, did not maintain their roles until the end of the period. By the late Edo period, only the Shibukawa, Yamaji, and Adachi families continued in the position.
In the 8th year of {{ill|Bunka|ja|文化}} (1811), {{ill|Takahashi Kageyasu|ja|高橋景保}} proposed establishing the {{ill|Bansho Wage Goyo|ja|蛮書和解御用}}, which lasted until the establishment of the {{ill|Institute for Western Studies|ja|蕃書調所}} in {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1858). By the late Edo period, the Tenmonkata also handled astronomical observations, surveying, geography, and the translation of Western books, serving as one of the predecessors of {{ill|The University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== The Tenmonkata Families ==
; Shibukawa Family : Owing to Shibukawa Shunkai's achievements, his family assumed the role of Tenmonkata in Jōkyō 1 (1684) and maintained the position, despite several adoptions, until the late Edo period. : {{ill|Shibukawa Shunkai|ja|渋川春海}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mukai|ja|渋川昔尹}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiin|ja|渋川敬尹}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiya|ja|渋川敬也}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Noriyasu|ja|渋川則休}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mitsuhiro|ja|渋川光洪}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masakiyo|ja|渋川正清}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masayō|ja|渋川正陽}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiyū|ja|渋川景佑}} – ({{ill|Shibukawa Keichoku|ja|渋川敬直}}) – {{ill|Shibukawa Sukemasa|ja|渋川佑賢}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiten|ja|渋川敬典}}
; Inokai Family : {{ill|Inokai Masakazu|ja|猪飼正一}} became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} 1 (1716). After his death in {{ill|Kanpō|ja|寛保}} 1 (1741), the family ceased its role.
; Nishikawa Family : Nishikawa Joken’s son, {{ill|Nishikawa Masayasu|ja|西川正休}}, was appointed in {{ill|Enkyō|ja|延享}} 4 (1747). The position lasted for two generations. : {{ill|Nishikawa Masayasu|ja|西川正休}} – {{ill|Nishikawa Tadataka|ja|西川忠喬}}
; Yamaji Family : {{ill|Yamaji Sukezumi|ja|山路主住}} became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Meiwa|ja|明和}} 1 (1764). His descendants continued until the end of the Edo period. : {{ill|Yamaji Sukezumi|ja|山路主住}} – ({{ill|Yamaji Yukiyuki|ja|山路之徽}}) – {{ill|Yamaji Tokufū|ja|山路徳風}} – {{ill|Yamaji Kanetaka|ja|山路諧孝}} – {{ill|Yamaji Akitsune|ja|山路彰常}} – ({{ill|Yamaji Akizumi|ja|山路彰善}})

It moved to {{ill|Kanda Surugadai|ja|神田駿河台}} in {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 14 (1701) and later to {{ill|Asakusa|ja|浅草}}. == Related Articles == {{div col}} * {{ill|Inō Tadataka|ja|伊能忠敬}} * {{ill|Mitsukuri Shūkichi|ja|箕作秋坪}} * {{ill|National Astronomical Observatory of Japan|ja|国立天文台}} {{div col end}}

{{Citation needed|date=July 2020}} '''Tenmonkata''' (天文方) was the astronomical and calendrical research institution established by the [[Tokugawa Shogunate]]. Its primary responsibility was overseeing the creation of calendars ({{ill|Henreki|ja|編暦}}).
== Overview ==
Initially, calendar creation was the responsibility of the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, a bureau under the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷}} and supervised by the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}}. However, in the first year of {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|貞享}} (1684), {{ill|Shibukawa Shunkai|ja|渋川春海}} created the {{ill|Jōkyō calendar|ja|貞享暦}}, replacing the traditional {{ill|Senmyō calendar|ja|宣明暦}}. Following this achievement, the Shogunate established the Tenmonkata under the supervision of the {{ill|Jisha Bugyō|ja|寺社奉行}}. On {{ill|December 1, 1684|ja|貞享元年12月1日}} (January 5, 1685), Shunkai was appointed the first Tenmonkata, which also became the name of the role. Calendar-making responsibilities shifted from the Imperial Court to the Shogunate, and the Tenmonkata carried out its tasks under Shogunate authority. Initially under the Jisha Bugyō, it was placed under the {{ill|Wakadoshiyori|ja|若年寄}} on {{ill|January 23, 1747|ja|延享4年1月23日}}.
The position was hereditary, but capable individuals with expertise in astronomy were occasionally appointed through adoption or special selection. By the end of the Edo period, eight families held the position: the {{ill|Shibukawa family|ja|渋川家}}, {{ill|Inokai family|ja|猪飼家}}, {{ill|Nishikawa family|ja|西川家}}, {{ill|Yamaji family|ja|山路家}}, {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}}, {{ill|Okumura family|ja|奥村家}}, {{ill|Takahashi family|ja|高橋家}}, and {{ill|Adachi family|ja|足立家}}. Some families, such as those of {{ill|Nishikawa Joken|ja|西川如見}} and {{ill|Takahashi Yoshitoki|ja|高橋至時}}, did not maintain their roles until the end of the period. By the late Edo period, only the Shibukawa, Yamaji, and Adachi families continued in the position.
In the 8th year of {{ill|Bunka|ja|文化}} (1811), {{ill|Takahashi Kageyasu|ja|高橋景保}} proposed establishing the {{ill|Bansho Wage Goyo|ja|蛮書和解御用}}, which lasted until the establishment of the {{ill|Institute for Western Studies|ja|蕃書調所}} in {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1858). By the late Edo period, the Tenmonkata also handled astronomical observations, surveying, geography, and the translation of Western books, serving as one of the predecessors of {{ill|The University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== The Tenmonkata Families ==
; Shibukawa Family : Owing to Shibukawa Shunkai's achievements, his family assumed the role of Tenmonkata in Jōkyō 1 (1684) and maintained the position, despite several adoptions, until the late Edo period. : {{ill|Shibukawa Shunkai|ja|渋川春海}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mukai|ja|渋川昔尹}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiin|ja|渋川敬尹}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiya|ja|渋川敬也}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Noriyasu|ja|渋川則休}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mitsuhiro|ja|渋川光洪}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masakiyo|ja|渋川正清}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masayō|ja|渋川正陽}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiyū|ja|渋川景佑}} – ({{ill|Shibukawa Keichoku|ja|渋川敬直}}) – {{ill|Shibukawa Sukemasa|ja|渋川佑賢}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiten|ja|渋川敬典}}
; Inokai Family : {{ill|Inokai Masakazu|ja|猪飼正一}} became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} 1 (1716). After his death in {{ill|Kanpō|ja|寛保}} 1 (1741), the family ceased its role.
; Nishikawa Family : Nishikawa Joken’s son, {{ill|Nishikawa Masayasu|ja|西川正休}}, was appointed in {{ill|Enkyō|ja|延享}} 4 (1747). The position lasted for two generations. : {{ill|Nishikawa Masayasu|ja|西川正休}} – {{ill|Nishikawa Tadataka|ja|西川忠喬}}
; Yamaji Family : {{ill|Yamaji Sukezumi|ja|山路主住}} became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Meiwa|ja|明和}} 1 (1764). His descendants continued until the end of the Edo period. : {{ill|Yamaji Sukezumi|ja|山路主住}} – ({{ill|Yamaji Yukiyuki|ja|山路之徽}}) – {{ill|Yamaji Tokufū|ja|山路徳風}} – {{ill|Yamaji Kanetaka|ja|山路諧孝}} – {{ill|Yamaji Akitsune|ja|山路彰常}} – ({{ill|Yamaji Akizumi|ja|山路彰善}})
; Yoshida Family : {{ill|Sasaki Nagahide|ja|佐々木長秀}}, later {{ill|Yoshida Nagahide|ja|吉田秀長}}, became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Meiwa|ja|明和}} 1 (1764). : {{ill|Yoshida Nagahide|ja|吉田秀長}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀升}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀賢}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀茂}}
== Observatories == The first observatory, '''Sitendai''', was established in {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|貞享}} 2 (1685) in {{ill|Ushigome Wakamachi|ja|牛込藁町}}. It moved to {{ill|Kanda Surugadai|ja|神田駿河台}} in {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 14 (1701) and later to {{ill|Asakusa|ja|浅草}}. In {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 13 (1842), a second observatory was established in {{ill|Kudanzaka|ja|九段坂}}. Both were abolished in {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 2 (1869).
== Abolition of the Tenmonkata == After the {{ill|Taisei Hōkan|ja|大政奉還}}, responsibility for calendrical work returned to the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}}. In {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 3 (1870), calendrical duties were transferred to the Ministry of Education's {{ill|Astronomical Bureau|ja|天文暦道局}}.
== Related Articles == {{div col}} * {{ill|Inō Tadataka|ja|伊能忠敬}} * {{ill|Mitsukuri Shūkichi|ja|箕作秋坪}} * {{ill|National Astronomical Observatory of Japan|ja|国立天文台}} {{div col end}}

== Overview ==
Initially, calendar creation was the responsibility of the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, a bureau under the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷}} and supervised by the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}}. However, in the first year of {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|貞享}} (1684), {{ill|Shibukawa Shunkai|ja|渋川春海}} created the {{ill|Jōkyō calendar|ja|貞享暦}}, replacing the traditional {{ill|Senmyō calendar|ja|宣明暦}}. Following this achievement, the Shogunate established the Tenmonkata under the supervision of the {{ill|Jisha Bugyō|ja|寺社奉行}}. On {{ill|December 1, 1684|ja|貞享元年12月1日}} (January 5, 1685), Shunkai was appointed the first Tenmonkata, which also became the name of the role. Calendar-making responsibilities shifted from the Imperial Court to the Shogunate, and the Tenmonkata carried out its tasks under Shogunate authority. Initially under the Jisha Bugyō, it was placed under the {{ill|Wakadoshiyori|ja|若年寄}} on {{ill|January 23, 1747|ja|延享4年1月23日}}.
The position was hereditary, but capable individuals with expertise in astronomy were occasionally appointed through adoption or special selection. By the end of the Edo period, eight families held the position: the {{ill|Shibukawa family|ja|渋川家}}, {{ill|Inokai family|ja|猪飼家}}, {{ill|Nishikawa family|ja|西川家}}, {{ill|Yamaji family|ja|山路家}}, {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}}, {{ill|Okumura family|ja|奥村家}}, {{ill|Takahashi family|ja|高橋家}}, and {{ill|Adachi family|ja|足立家}}. Some families, such as those of {{ill|Nishikawa Joken|ja|西川如見}} and {{ill|Takahashi Yoshitoki|ja|高橋至時}}, did not maintain their roles until the end of the period. By the late Edo period, only the Shibukawa, Yamaji, and Adachi families continued in the position.
In the 8th year of {{ill|Bunka|ja|文化}} (1811), {{ill|Takahashi Kageyasu|ja|高橋景保}} proposed establishing the {{ill|Bansho Wage Goyo|ja|蛮書和解御用}}, which lasted until the establishment of the {{ill|Institute for Western Studies|ja|蕃書調所}} in {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1858). By the late Edo period, the Tenmonkata also handled astronomical observations, surveying, geography, and the translation of Western books, serving as one of the predecessors of {{ill|The University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== The Tenmonkata Families ==
; Shibukawa Family : Owing to Shibukawa Shunkai's achievements, his family assumed the role of Tenmonkata in Jōkyō 1 (1684) and maintained the position, despite several adoptions, until the late Edo period. : {{ill|Shibukawa Shunkai|ja|渋川春海}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mukai|ja|渋川昔尹}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiin|ja|渋川敬尹}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiya|ja|渋川敬也}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Noriyasu|ja|渋川則休}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mitsuhiro|ja|渋川光洪}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masakiyo|ja|渋川正清}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masayō|ja|渋川正陽}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiyū|ja|渋川景佑}} – ({{ill|Shibukawa Keichoku|ja|渋川敬直}}) – {{ill|Shibukawa Sukemasa|ja|渋川佑賢}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiten|ja|渋川敬典}}
; Inokai Family : {{ill|Inokai Masakazu|ja|猪飼正一}} became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} 1 (1716). After his death in {{ill|Kanpō|ja|寛保}} 1 (1741), the family ceased its role.
; Nishikawa Family : Nishikawa Joken’s son, {{ill|Nishikawa Masayasu|ja|西川正休}}, was appointed in {{ill|Enkyō|ja|延享}} 4 (1747). The position lasted for two generations. : {{ill|Nishikawa Masayasu|ja|西川正休}} – {{ill|Nishikawa Tadataka|ja|西川忠喬}}
; Yamaji Family : {{ill|Yamaji Sukezumi|ja|山路主住}} became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Meiwa|ja|明和}} 1 (1764). His descendants continued until the end of the Edo period. : {{ill|Yamaji Sukezumi|ja|山路主住}} – ({{ill|Yamaji Yukiyuki|ja|山路之徽}}) – {{ill|Yamaji Tokufū|ja|山路徳風}} – {{ill|Yamaji Kanetaka|ja|山路諧孝}} – {{ill|Yamaji Akitsune|ja|山路彰常}} – ({{ill|Yamaji Akizumi|ja|山路彰善}})
; Yoshida Family : {{ill|Sasaki Nagahide|ja|佐々木長秀}}, later {{ill|Yoshida Nagahide|ja|吉田秀長}}, became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Meiwa|ja|明和}} 1 (1764). : {{ill|Yoshida Nagahide|ja|吉田秀長}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀升}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀賢}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀茂}}
== Observatories == The first observatory, '''Sitendai''', was established in {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|貞享}} 2 (1685) in {{ill|Ushigome Wakamachi|ja|牛込藁町}}. It moved to {{ill|Kanda Surugadai|ja|神田駿河台}} in {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 14 (1701) and later to {{ill|Asakusa|ja|浅草}}. In {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 13 (1842), a second observatory was established in {{ill|Kudanzaka|ja|九段坂}}. Both were abolished in {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 2 (1869).
== Abolition of the Tenmonkata == After the {{ill|Taisei Hōkan|ja|大政奉還}}, responsibility for calendrical work returned to the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}}. In {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 3 (1870), calendrical duties were transferred to the Ministry of Education's {{ill|Astronomical Bureau|ja|天文暦道局}}.
== Related Articles == {{div col}} * {{ill|Inō Tadataka|ja|伊能忠敬}} * {{ill|Mitsukuri Shūkichi|ja|箕作秋坪}} * {{ill|National Astronomical Observatory of Japan|ja|国立天文台}} {{div col end}}
== External Links == * {{Kotobank|Tenmonkata|2=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)}} [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tenmongata]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tenmongata]][[Category:11]] {{Citation needed|date=July 2020}} '''Tenmonkata''' (天文方) was the astronomical and calendrical research institution established by the [[Tokugawa Shogunate]]. Its primary responsibility was overseeing the creation of calendars ({{ill|Henreki|ja|編暦}}).
== Overview ==
Initially, calendar creation was the responsibility of the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, a bureau under the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷}} and supervised by the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}}. However, in the first year of {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|貞享}} (1684), {{ill|Shibukawa Shunkai|ja|渋川春海}} created the {{ill|Jōkyō calendar|ja|貞享暦}}, replacing the traditional {{ill|Senmyō calendar|ja|宣明暦}}. Following this achievement, the Shogunate established the Tenmonkata under the supervision of the {{ill|Jisha Bugyō|ja|寺社奉行}}. On {{ill|December 1, 1684|ja|貞享元年12月1日}} (January 5, 1685), Shunkai was appointed the first Tenmonkata, which also became the name of the role. Calendar-making responsibilities shifted from the Imperial Court to the Shogunate, and the Tenmonkata carried out its tasks under Shogunate authority. Initially under the Jisha Bugyō, it was placed under the {{ill|Wakadoshiyori|ja|若年寄}} on {{ill|January 23, 1747|ja|延享4年1月23日}}.
The position was hereditary, but capable individuals with expertise in astronomy were occasionally appointed through adoption or special selection. By the end of the Edo period, eight families held the position: the {{ill|Shibukawa family|ja|渋川家}}, {{ill|Inokai family|ja|猪飼家}}, {{ill|Nishikawa family|ja|西川家}}, {{ill|Yamaji family|ja|山路家}}, {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}}, {{ill|Okumura family|ja|奥村家}}, {{ill|Takahashi family|ja|高橋家}}, and {{ill|Adachi family|ja|足立家}}. Some families, such as those of {{ill|Nishikawa Joken|ja|西川如見}} and {{ill|Takahashi Yoshitoki|ja|高橋至時}}, did not maintain their roles until the end of the period. By the late Edo period, only the Shibukawa, Yamaji, and Adachi families continued in the position.
In the 8th year of {{ill|Bunka|ja|文化}} (1811), {{ill|Takahashi Kageyasu|ja|高橋景保}} proposed establishing the {{ill|Bansho Wage Goyo|ja|蛮書和解御用}}, which lasted until the establishment of the {{ill|Institute for Western Studies|ja|蕃書調所}} in {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1858). By the late Edo period, the Tenmonkata also handled astronomical observations, surveying, geography, and the translation of Western books, serving as one of the predecessors of {{ill|The University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== The Tenmonkata Families ==
; Shibukawa Family : Owing to Shibukawa Shunkai's achievements, his family assumed the role of Tenmonkata in Jōkyō 1 (1684) and maintained the position, despite several adoptions, until the late Edo period. : {{ill|Shibukawa Shunkai|ja|渋川春海}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mukai|ja|渋川昔尹}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiin|ja|渋川敬尹}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiya|ja|渋川敬也}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Noriyasu|ja|渋川則休}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mitsuhiro|ja|渋川光洪}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masakiyo|ja|渋川正清}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masayō|ja|渋川正陽}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiyū|ja|渋川景佑}} – ({{ill|Shibukawa Keichoku|ja|渋川敬直}}) – {{ill|Shibukawa Sukemasa|ja|渋川佑賢}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiten|ja|渋川敬典}}
; Inokai Family : {{ill|Inokai Masakazu|ja|猪飼正一}} became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} 1 (1716). After his death in {{ill|Kanpō|ja|寛保}} 1 (1741), the family ceased its role.
; Nishikawa Family : Nishikawa Joken’s son, {{ill|Nishikawa Masayasu|ja|西川正休}}, was appointed in {{ill|Enkyō|ja|延享}} 4 (1747). The position lasted for two generations. : {{ill|Nishikawa Masayasu|ja|西川正休}} – {{ill|Nishikawa Tadataka|ja|西川忠喬}}
; Yamaji Family : {{ill|Yamaji Sukezumi|ja|山路主住}} became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Meiwa|ja|明和}} 1 (1764). His descendants continued until the end of the Edo period. : {{ill|Yamaji Sukezumi|ja|山路主住}} – ({{ill|Yamaji Yukiyuki|ja|山路之徽}}) – {{ill|Yamaji Tokufū|ja|山路徳風}} – {{ill|Yamaji Kanetaka|ja|山路諧孝}} – {{ill|Yamaji Akitsune|ja|山路彰常}} – ({{ill|Yamaji Akizumi|ja|山路彰善}})
; Yoshida Family : {{ill|Sasaki Nagahide|ja|佐々木長秀}}, later {{ill|Yoshida Nagahide|ja|吉田秀長}}, became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Meiwa|ja|明和}} 1 (1764). : {{ill|Yoshida Nagahide|ja|吉田秀長}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀升}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀賢}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀茂}}
== Observatories == The first observatory, '''Sitendai''', was established in {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|貞享}} 2 (1685) in {{ill|Ushigome Wakamachi|ja|牛込藁町}}. It moved to {{ill|Kanda Surugadai|ja|神田駿河台}} in {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 14 (1701) and later to {{ill|Asakusa|ja|浅草}}. In {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 13 (1842), a second observatory was established in {{ill|Kudanzaka|ja|九段坂}}. Both were abolished in {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 2 (1869).
== Abolition of the Tenmonkata == After the {{ill|Taisei Hōkan|ja|大政奉還}}, responsibility for calendrical work returned to the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}}. In {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 3 (1870), calendrical duties were transferred to the Ministry of Education's {{ill|Astronomical Bureau|ja|天文暦道局}}.
== Related Articles == {{div col}} * {{ill|Inō Tadataka|ja|伊能忠敬}} * {{ill|Mitsukuri Shūkichi|ja|箕作秋坪}} * {{ill|National Astronomical Observatory of Japan|ja|国立天文台}} {{div col end}}

; Shibukawa Family : Owing to Shibukawa Shunkai's achievements, his family assumed the role of Tenmonkata in Jōkyō 1 (1684) and maintained the position, despite several adoptions, until the late Edo period. : {{ill|Shibukawa Shunkai|ja|渋川春海}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mukai|ja|渋川昔尹}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiin|ja|渋川敬尹}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiya|ja|渋川敬也}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Noriyasu|ja|渋川則休}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mitsuhiro|ja|渋川光洪}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masakiyo|ja|渋川正清}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masayō|ja|渋川正陽}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiyū|ja|渋川景佑}} – ({{ill|Shibukawa Keichoku|ja|渋川敬直}}) – {{ill|Shibukawa Sukemasa|ja|渋川佑賢}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiten|ja|渋川敬典}}
; Inokai Family : {{ill|Inokai Masakazu|ja|猪飼正一}} became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} 1 (1716). After his death in {{ill|Kanpō|ja|寛保}} 1 (1741), the family ceased its role.
; Nishikawa Family : Nishikawa Joken’s son, {{ill|Nishikawa Masayasu|ja|西川正休}}, was appointed in {{ill|Enkyō|ja|延享}} 4 (1747). The position lasted for two generations. : {{ill|Nishikawa Masayasu|ja|西川正休}} – {{ill|Nishikawa Tadataka|ja|西川忠喬}}
; Yamaji Family : {{ill|Yamaji Sukezumi|ja|山路主住}} became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Meiwa|ja|明和}} 1 (1764). His descendants continued until the end of the Edo period. : {{ill|Yamaji Sukezumi|ja|山路主住}} – ({{ill|Yamaji Yukiyuki|ja|山路之徽}}) – {{ill|Yamaji Tokufū|ja|山路徳風}} – {{ill|Yamaji Kanetaka|ja|山路諧孝}} – {{ill|Yamaji Akitsune|ja|山路彰常}} – ({{ill|Yamaji Akizumi|ja|山路彰善}})
; Yoshida Family : {{ill|Sasaki Nagahide|ja|佐々木長秀}}, later {{ill|Yoshida Nagahide|ja|吉田秀長}}, became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Meiwa|ja|明和}} 1 (1764). : {{ill|Yoshida Nagahide|ja|吉田秀長}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀升}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀賢}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀茂}}
== Observatories == The first observatory, '''Sitendai''', was established in {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|貞享}} 2 (1685) in {{ill|Ushigome Wakamachi|ja|牛込藁町}}. It moved to {{ill|Kanda Surugadai|ja|神田駿河台}} in {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 14 (1701) and later to {{ill|Asakusa|ja|浅草}}. In {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 13 (1842), a second observatory was established in {{ill|Kudanzaka|ja|九段坂}}. Both were abolished in {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 2 (1869).
== Abolition of the Tenmonkata == After the {{ill|Taisei Hōkan|ja|大政奉還}}, responsibility for calendrical work returned to the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}}. In {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 3 (1870), calendrical duties were transferred to the Ministry of Education's {{ill|Astronomical Bureau|ja|天文暦道局}}.

{{Citation needed|date=July 2020}} '''Tenmonkata''' (天文方) was the astronomical and calendrical research institution established by the [[Tokugawa Shogunate]]. Its primary responsibility was overseeing the creation of calendars ({{ill|Henreki|ja|編暦}}).
== Overview ==
Initially, calendar creation was the responsibility of the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, a bureau under the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷}} and supervised by the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}}. However, in the first year of {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|貞享}} (1684), {{ill|Shibukawa Shunkai|ja|渋川春海}} created the {{ill|Jōkyō calendar|ja|貞享暦}}, replacing the traditional {{ill|Senmyō calendar|ja|宣明暦}}. Following this achievement, the Shogunate established the Tenmonkata under the supervision of the {{ill|Jisha Bugyō|ja|寺社奉行}}. On {{ill|December 1, 1684|ja|貞享元年12月1日}} (January 5, 1685), Shunkai was appointed the first Tenmonkata, which also became the name of the role. Calendar-making responsibilities shifted from the Imperial Court to the Shogunate, and the Tenmonkata carried out its tasks under Shogunate authority. Initially under the Jisha Bugyō, it was placed under the {{ill|Wakadoshiyori|ja|若年寄}} on {{ill|January 23, 1747|ja|延享4年1月23日}}.
The position was hereditary, but capable individuals with expertise in astronomy were occasionally appointed through adoption or special selection. By the end of the Edo period, eight families held the position: the {{ill|Shibukawa family|ja|渋川家}}, {{ill|Inokai family|ja|猪飼家}}, {{ill|Nishikawa family|ja|西川家}}, {{ill|Yamaji family|ja|山路家}}, {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}}, {{ill|Okumura family|ja|奥村家}}, {{ill|Takahashi family|ja|高橋家}}, and {{ill|Adachi family|ja|足立家}}. Some families, such as those of {{ill|Nishikawa Joken|ja|西川如見}} and {{ill|Takahashi Yoshitoki|ja|高橋至時}}, did not maintain their roles until the end of the period. By the late Edo period, only the Shibukawa, Yamaji, and Adachi families continued in the position.
In the 8th year of {{ill|Bunka|ja|文化}} (1811), {{ill|Takahashi Kageyasu|ja|高橋景保}} proposed establishing the {{ill|Bansho Wage Goyo|ja|蛮書和解御用}}, which lasted until the establishment of the {{ill|Institute for Western Studies|ja|蕃書調所}} in {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1858). By the late Edo period, the Tenmonkata also handled astronomical observations, surveying, geography, and the translation of Western books, serving as one of the predecessors of {{ill|The University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== The Tenmonkata Families ==
; Shibukawa Family : Owing to Shibukawa Shunkai's achievements, his family assumed the role of Tenmonkata in Jōkyō 1 (1684) and maintained the position, despite several adoptions, until the late Edo period. : {{ill|Shibukawa Shunkai|ja|渋川春海}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mukai|ja|渋川昔尹}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiin|ja|渋川敬尹}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiya|ja|渋川敬也}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Noriyasu|ja|渋川則休}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mitsuhiro|ja|渋川光洪}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masakiyo|ja|渋川正清}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masayō|ja|渋川正陽}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiyū|ja|渋川景佑}} – ({{ill|Shibukawa Keichoku|ja|渋川敬直}}) – {{ill|Shibukawa Sukemasa|ja|渋川佑賢}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiten|ja|渋川敬典}}
; Inokai Family : {{ill|Inokai Masakazu|ja|猪飼正一}} became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} 1 (1716). After his death in {{ill|Kanpō|ja|寛保}} 1 (1741), the family ceased its role.
; Nishikawa Family : Nishikawa Joken’s son, {{ill|Nishikawa Masayasu|ja|西川正休}}, was appointed in {{ill|Enkyō|ja|延享}} 4 (1747). The position lasted for two generations. : {{ill|Nishikawa Masayasu|ja|西川正休}} – {{ill|Nishikawa Tadataka|ja|西川忠喬}}
; Yamaji Family : {{ill|Yamaji Sukezumi|ja|山路主住}} became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Meiwa|ja|明和}} 1 (1764). His descendants continued until the end of the Edo period. : {{ill|Yamaji Sukezumi|ja|山路主住}} – ({{ill|Yamaji Yukiyuki|ja|山路之徽}}) – {{ill|Yamaji Tokufū|ja|山路徳風}} – {{ill|Yamaji Kanetaka|ja|山路諧孝}} – {{ill|Yamaji Akitsune|ja|山路彰常}} – ({{ill|Yamaji Akizumi|ja|山路彰善}})
; Yoshida Family : {{ill|Sasaki Nagahide|ja|佐々木長秀}}, later {{ill|Yoshida Nagahide|ja|吉田秀長}}, became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Meiwa|ja|明和}} 1 (1764). : {{ill|Yoshida Nagahide|ja|吉田秀長}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀升}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀賢}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀茂}}
== Observatories == The first observatory, '''Sitendai''', was established in {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|貞享}} 2 (1685) in {{ill|Ushigome Wakamachi|ja|牛込藁町}}. It moved to {{ill|Kanda Surugadai|ja|神田駿河台}} in {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 14 (1701) and later to {{ill|Asakusa|ja|浅草}}. In {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 13 (1842), a second observatory was established in {{ill|Kudanzaka|ja|九段坂}}. Both were abolished in {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 2 (1869).
== Abolition of the Tenmonkata == After the {{ill|Taisei Hōkan|ja|大政奉還}}, responsibility for calendrical work returned to the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}}. In {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 3 (1870), calendrical duties were transferred to the Ministry of Education's {{ill|Astronomical Bureau|ja|天文暦道局}}.

In {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 13 (1842), a second observatory was established in {{ill|Kudanzaka|ja|九段坂}}. Both were abolished in {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 2 (1869).
== Abolition of the Tenmonkata == After the {{ill|Taisei Hōkan|ja|大政奉還}}, responsibility for calendrical work returned to the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}}. In {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 3 (1870), calendrical duties were transferred to the Ministry of Education's {{ill|Astronomical Bureau|ja|天文暦道局}}.

{{Citation needed|date=July 2020}} '''Tenmonkata''' (天文方) was the astronomical and calendrical research institution established by the [[Tokugawa Shogunate]]. Its primary responsibility was overseeing the creation of calendars ({{ill|Henreki|ja|編暦}}).
== Overview ==
Initially, calendar creation was the responsibility of the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, a bureau under the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷}} and supervised by the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}}. However, in the first year of {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|貞享}} (1684), {{ill|Shibukawa Shunkai|ja|渋川春海}} created the {{ill|Jōkyō calendar|ja|貞享暦}}, replacing the traditional {{ill|Senmyō calendar|ja|宣明暦}}. Following this achievement, the Shogunate established the Tenmonkata under the supervision of the {{ill|Jisha Bugyō|ja|寺社奉行}}. On {{ill|December 1, 1684|ja|貞享元年12月1日}} (January 5, 1685), Shunkai was appointed the first Tenmonkata, which also became the name of the role. Calendar-making responsibilities shifted from the Imperial Court to the Shogunate, and the Tenmonkata carried out its tasks under Shogunate authority. Initially under the Jisha Bugyō, it was placed under the {{ill|Wakadoshiyori|ja|若年寄}} on {{ill|January 23, 1747|ja|延享4年1月23日}}.
The position was hereditary, but capable individuals with expertise in astronomy were occasionally appointed through adoption or special selection. By the end of the Edo period, eight families held the position: the {{ill|Shibukawa family|ja|渋川家}}, {{ill|Inokai family|ja|猪飼家}}, {{ill|Nishikawa family|ja|西川家}}, {{ill|Yamaji family|ja|山路家}}, {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}}, {{ill|Okumura family|ja|奥村家}}, {{ill|Takahashi family|ja|高橋家}}, and {{ill|Adachi family|ja|足立家}}. Some families, such as those of {{ill|Nishikawa Joken|ja|西川如見}} and {{ill|Takahashi Yoshitoki|ja|高橋至時}}, did not maintain their roles until the end of the period. By the late Edo period, only the Shibukawa, Yamaji, and Adachi families continued in the position.
In the 8th year of {{ill|Bunka|ja|文化}} (1811), {{ill|Takahashi Kageyasu|ja|高橋景保}} proposed establishing the {{ill|Bansho Wage Goyo|ja|蛮書和解御用}}, which lasted until the establishment of the {{ill|Institute for Western Studies|ja|蕃書調所}} in {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1858). By the late Edo period, the Tenmonkata also handled astronomical observations, surveying, geography, and the translation of Western books, serving as one of the predecessors of {{ill|The University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== The Tenmonkata Families ==
; Shibukawa Family : Owing to Shibukawa Shunkai's achievements, his family assumed the role of Tenmonkata in Jōkyō 1 (1684) and maintained the position, despite several adoptions, until the late Edo period. : {{ill|Shibukawa Shunkai|ja|渋川春海}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mukai|ja|渋川昔尹}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiin|ja|渋川敬尹}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiya|ja|渋川敬也}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Noriyasu|ja|渋川則休}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mitsuhiro|ja|渋川光洪}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masakiyo|ja|渋川正清}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masayō|ja|渋川正陽}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiyū|ja|渋川景佑}} – ({{ill|Shibukawa Keichoku|ja|渋川敬直}}) – {{ill|Shibukawa Sukemasa|ja|渋川佑賢}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiten|ja|渋川敬典}}
; Inokai Family : {{ill|Inokai Masakazu|ja|猪飼正一}} became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} 1 (1716). After his death in {{ill|Kanpō|ja|寛保}} 1 (1741), the family ceased its role.
; Nishikawa Family : Nishikawa Joken’s son, {{ill|Nishikawa Masayasu|ja|西川正休}}, was appointed in {{ill|Enkyō|ja|延享}} 4 (1747). The position lasted for two generations. : {{ill|Nishikawa Masayasu|ja|西川正休}} – {{ill|Nishikawa Tadataka|ja|西川忠喬}}
; Yamaji Family : {{ill|Yamaji Sukezumi|ja|山路主住}} became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Meiwa|ja|明和}} 1 (1764). His descendants continued until the end of the Edo period. : {{ill|Yamaji Sukezumi|ja|山路主住}} – ({{ill|Yamaji Yukiyuki|ja|山路之徽}}) – {{ill|Yamaji Tokufū|ja|山路徳風}} – {{ill|Yamaji Kanetaka|ja|山路諧孝}} – {{ill|Yamaji Akitsune|ja|山路彰常}} – ({{ill|Yamaji Akizumi|ja|山路彰善}})
; Yoshida Family : {{ill|Sasaki Nagahide|ja|佐々木長秀}}, later {{ill|Yoshida Nagahide|ja|吉田秀長}}, became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Meiwa|ja|明和}} 1 (1764). : {{ill|Yoshida Nagahide|ja|吉田秀長}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀升}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀賢}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀茂}}
== Observatories == The first observatory, '''Sitendai''', was established in {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|貞享}} 2 (1685) in {{ill|Ushigome Wakamachi|ja|牛込藁町}}. It moved to {{ill|Kanda Surugadai|ja|神田駿河台}} in {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 14 (1701) and later to {{ill|Asakusa|ja|浅草}}. In {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 13 (1842), a second observatory was established in {{ill|Kudanzaka|ja|九段坂}}. Both were abolished in {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 2 (1869).
== Abolition of the Tenmonkata == After the {{ill|Taisei Hōkan|ja|大政奉還}}, responsibility for calendrical work returned to the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}}. In {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 3 (1870), calendrical duties were transferred to the Ministry of Education's {{ill|Astronomical Bureau|ja|天文暦道局}}.
== Related Articles == {{div col}} * {{ill|Inō Tadataka|ja|伊能忠敬}} * {{ill|Mitsukuri Shūkichi|ja|箕作秋坪}} * {{ill|National Astronomical Observatory of Japan|ja|国立天文台}} {{div col end}}

{{Citation needed|date=July 2020}} '''Tenmonkata''' (天文方) was the astronomical and calendrical research institution established by the [[Tokugawa Shogunate]]. Its primary responsibility was overseeing the creation of calendars ({{ill|Henreki|ja|編暦}}).
== Overview ==
Initially, calendar creation was the responsibility of the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, a bureau under the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷}} and supervised by the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}}. However, in the first year of {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|貞享}} (1684), {{ill|Shibukawa Shunkai|ja|渋川春海}} created the {{ill|Jōkyō calendar|ja|貞享暦}}, replacing the traditional {{ill|Senmyō calendar|ja|宣明暦}}. Following this achievement, the Shogunate established the Tenmonkata under the supervision of the {{ill|Jisha Bugyō|ja|寺社奉行}}. On {{ill|December 1, 1684|ja|貞享元年12月1日}} (January 5, 1685), Shunkai was appointed the first Tenmonkata, which also became the name of the role. Calendar-making responsibilities shifted from the Imperial Court to the Shogunate, and the Tenmonkata carried out its tasks under Shogunate authority. Initially under the Jisha Bugyō, it was placed under the {{ill|Wakadoshiyori|ja|若年寄}} on {{ill|January 23, 1747|ja|延享4th year 1月23日}}.
The position was hereditary, but capable individuals with expertise in astronomy were occasionally appointed through adoption or special selection. By the end of the Edo period, eight families held the position: the {{ill|Shibukawa family|ja|渋川家}}, {{ill|Inokai family|ja|猪飼家}}, {{ill|Nishikawa family|ja|西川家}}, {{ill|Yamaji family|ja|山路家}}, {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}}, {{ill|Okumura family|ja|奥村家}}, {{ill|Takahashi family|ja|高橋家}}, and {{ill|Adachi family|ja|足立家}}. Some families, such as those of {{ill|Nishikawa Joken|ja|西川如見}} and {{ill|Takahashi Yoshitoki|ja|高橋至時}}, did not maintain their roles until the end of the period. By the late Edo period, only the Shibukawa, Yamaji, and Adachi families continued in the position.
In the 8th year of {{ill|Bunka|ja|文化}} (1811), {{ill|Takahashi Kageyasu|ja|高橋景保}} proposed establishing the {{ill|Bansho Wage Goyo|ja|蛮書和解御用}}, which lasted until the establishment of the {{ill|Institute for Western Studies|ja|蕃書調所}} in {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1858). By the late Edo period, the Tenmonkata also handled astronomical observations, surveying, geography, and the translation of Western books, serving as one of the predecessors of {{ill|The University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== The Tenmonkata Families ==
; Shibukawa Family : Owing to Shibukawa Shunkai's achievements, his family assumed the role of Tenmonkata in Jōkyō 1 (1684) and maintained the position, despite several adoptions, until the late Edo period. : {{ill|Shibukawa Shunkai|ja|渋川春海}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mukai|ja|渋川昔尹}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiin|ja|渋川敬尹}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiya|ja|渋川敬也}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Noriyasu|ja|渋川則休}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mitsuhiro|ja|渋川光洪}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masakiyo|ja|渋川正清}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masayō|ja|渋川正陽}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiyū|ja|渋川景佑}} – ({{ill|Shibukawa Keichoku|ja|渋川敬直}}) – {{ill|Shibukawa Sukemasa|ja|渋川佑賢}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiten|ja|渋川敬典}}
; Inokai Family : {{ill|Inokai Masakazu|ja|猪飼正一}} became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} 1 (1716). After his death in {{ill|Kanpō|ja|寛保}} 1 (1741), the family ceased its role.
; Nishikawa Family : Nishikawa Joken’s son, {{ill|Nishikawa Masayasu|ja|西川正休}}, was appointed in {{ill|Enkyō|ja|延享}} 4 (1747). The position lasted for two generations. : {{ill|Nishikawa Masayasu|ja|西川正休}} – {{ill|Nishikawa Tadataka|ja|西川忠喬}}
; Yamaji Family : {{ill|Yamaji Sukezumi|ja|山路主住}} became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Meiwa|ja|明和}} 1 (1764). His descendants continued until the end of the Edo period. : {{ill|Yamaji Sukezumi|ja|山路主住}} – ({{ill|Yamaji Yukiyuki|ja|山路之徽}}) – {{ill|Yamaji Tokufū|ja|山路徳風}} – {{ill|Yamaji Kanetaka|ja|山路諧孝}} – {{ill|Yamaji Akitsune|ja|山路彰常}} – ({{ill|Yamaji Akizumi|ja|山路彰善}})
; Yoshida Family : {{ill|Sasaki Nagahide|ja|佐々木長秀}}, later {{ill|Yoshida Nagahide|ja|吉田秀長}}, became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Meiwa|ja|明和}} 1 (1764). : {{ill|Yoshida Nagahide|ja|吉田秀長}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀升}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀賢}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀茂}}
== Observatories == The first observatory, '''Sitendai''', was established in {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|貞享}} 2 (1685) in {{ill|Ushigome Wakamachi|ja|牛込藁町}}. It moved to {{ill|Kanda Surugadai|ja|神田駿河台}} in {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 14 (1701) and later to {{ill|Asakusa|ja|浅草}}. In {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 13 (1842), a second observatory was established in {{ill|Kudanzaka|ja|九段坂}}. Both were abolished in {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 2 (1869).
== Abolition of the Tenmonkata == After the {{ill|Taisei Hōkan|ja|大政奉還}}, responsibility for calendrical work returned to the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}}. In {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 3 (1870), calendrical duties were transferred to the Ministry of Education's {{ill|Astronomical Bureau|ja|天文暦道局}}.
== Related Articles == {{div col}} * {{ill|Inō Tadataka|ja|伊能忠敬}} * {{ill|Mitsukuri Shūkichi|ja|箕作秋坪}} * {{ill|National Astronomical Observatory of Japan|ja|国立天文台}} {{div col end}}

{{Citation needed|date=July 2020}} '''Tenmonkata''' (天文方) was the astronomical and calendrical research institution established by the [[Tokugawa Shogunate]]. Its primary responsibility was overseeing the creation of calendars ({{ill|Henreki|ja|編暦}}).
== Overview ==
Initially, calendar creation was the responsibility of the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, a bureau under the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷}} and supervised by the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}}. However, in the first year of {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|貞享}} (1684), {{ill|Shibukawa Shunkai|ja|渋川春海}} created the {{ill|Jōkyō calendar|ja|貞享暦}}, replacing the traditional {{ill|Senmyō calendar|ja|宣明暦}}. Following this achievement, the Shogunate established the Tenmonkata under the supervision of the {{ill|Jisha Bugyō|ja|寺社奉行}}. On {{ill|December 1, 1684|ja|貞享元年12月1日}} (January 5, 1685), Shunkai was appointed the first Tenmonkata, which also became the name of the role. Calendar-making responsibilities shifted from the Imperial Court to the Shogunate, and the Tenmonkata carried out its tasks under Shogunate authority. Initially under the Jisha Bugyō, it was placed under the {{ill|Wakadoshiyori|ja|若年寄}} on {{ill|January 23, 1747|ja|延享4th year 1月23日}}.
The position was hereditary, but capable individuals with expertise in astronomy were occasionally appointed through adoption or special selection. By the end of the Edo period, eight families held the position: the {{ill|Shibukawa family|ja|渋川家}}, {{ill|Inokai family|ja|猪飼家}}, {{ill|Nishikawa family|ja|西川家}}, {{ill|Yamaji family|ja|山路家}}, {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家}}, {{ill|Okumura family|ja|奥村家}}, {{ill|Takahashi family|ja|高橋家}}, and {{ill|Adachi family|ja|足立家}}. Some families, such as those of {{ill|Nishikawa Joken|ja|西川如見}} and {{ill|Takahashi Yoshitoki|ja|高橋至時}}, did not maintain their roles until the end of the period. By the late Edo period, only the Shibukawa, Yamaji, and Adachi families continued in the position.
In the 8th year of {{ill|Bunka|ja|文化}} (1811), {{ill|Takahashi Kageyasu|ja|高橋景保}} proposed establishing the {{ill|Bansho Wage Goyo|ja|蛮書和解御用}}, which lasted until the establishment of the {{ill|Institute for Western Studies|ja|蕃書調所}} in {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 3 (1858). By the late Edo period, the Tenmonkata also handled astronomical observations, surveying, geography, and the translation of Western books, serving as one of the predecessors of {{ill|The University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学}}.
== The Tenmonkata Families ==
; Shibukawa Family : Owing to Shibukawa Shunkai's achievements, his family assumed the role of Tenmonkata in Jōkyō 1 (1684) and maintained the position, despite several adoptions, until the late Edo period. : {{ill|Shibukawa Shunkai|ja|渋川春海}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mukai|ja|渋川昔尹}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiin|ja|渋川敬尹}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiya|ja|渋川敬也}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Noriyasu|ja|渋川則休}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mitsuhiro|ja|渋川光洪}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masakiyo|ja|渋川正清}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masayō|ja|渋川正陽}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiyū|ja|渋川景佑}} – ({{ill|Shibukawa Keichoku|ja|渋川敬直}}) – {{ill|Shibukawa Sukemasa|ja|渋川佑賢}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiten|ja|渋川敬典}}
; Inokai Family : {{ill|Inokai Masakazu|ja|猪飼正一}} became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} 1 (1716). After his death in {{ill|Kanpō|ja|寛保}} 1 (1741), the family ceased its role.
; Nishikawa Family : Nishikawa Joken’s son, {{ill|Nishikawa Masayasu|ja|西川正休}}, was appointed in {{ill|Enkyō|ja|延享}} 4 (1747). The position lasted for two generations. : {{ill|Nishikawa Masayasu|ja|西川正休}} – {{ill|Nishikawa Tadataka|ja|西川忠喬}}
; Yamaji Family : {{ill|Yamaji Sukezumi|ja|山路主住}} became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Meiwa|ja|明和}} 1 (1764). His descendants continued until the end of the Edo period. : {{ill|Yamaji Sukezumi|ja|山路主住}} – ({{ill|Yamaji Yukiyuki|ja|山路之徽}}) – {{ill|Yamaji Tokufū|ja|山路徳風}} – {{ill|Yamaji Kanetaka|ja|山路諧孝}} – {{ill|Yamaji Akitsune|ja|山路彰常}} – ({{ill|Yamaji Akizumi|ja|山路彰善}})
; Yoshida Family : {{ill|Sasaki Nagahide|ja|佐々木長秀}}, later {{ill|Yoshida Nagahide|ja|吉田秀長}}, became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Meiwa|ja|明和}} 1 (1764). : {{ill|Yoshida Nagahide|ja|吉田秀長}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀升}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀賢}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀茂}}
== Observatories == The first observatory, '''Sitendai''', was established in {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|貞享}} 2 (1685) in {{ill|Ushigome Wakamachi|ja|牛込藁町}}. It moved to {{ill|Kanda Surugadai|ja|神田駿河台}} in {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 14 (1701) and later to {{ill|Asakusa|ja|浅草}}. In {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 13 (1842), a second observatory was established in {{ill|Kudanzaka|ja|九段坂}}. Both were abolished in {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 2 (1869).
== Abolition of the Tenmonkata == After the {{ill|Taisei Hōkan|ja|大政奉還}}, responsibility for calendrical work returned to the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}}. In {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 3 (1870), calendrical duties were transferred to the Ministry of Education's {{ill|Astronomical Bureau|ja|天文暦道局}}.
== Related Articles == {{div col}} * {{ill|Inō Tadataka|ja|伊能忠敬}} * {{ill|Mitsukuri Shūkichi|ja|箕作秋坪}} * {{ill|National Astronomical Observatory of Japan|ja|国立天文台}} {{div col end}}
== External Links == * {{Kotobank|Tenmonkata|2=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)}}

; Shibukawa Family : Owing to Shibukawa Shunkai's achievements, his family assumed the role of Tenmonkata in Jōkyō 1 (1684) and maintained the position, despite several adoptions, until the late Edo period. : {{ill|Shibukawa Shunkai|ja|渋川春海|12=draft}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mukai|ja|渋川昔尹|12=draft}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiin|ja|渋川敬尹|12=draft}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiya|ja|渋川敬也|12=draft}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Noriyasu|ja|渋川則休|12=draft}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mitsuhiro|ja|渋川光洪|12=draft}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masakiyo|ja|渋川正清|12=draft}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masayō|ja|渋川正陽|12=draft}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiyū|ja|渋川景佑|12=draft}} – ({{ill|Shibukawa Keichoku|ja|渋川敬直|12=draft}}) – {{ill|Shibukawa Sukemasa|ja|渋川佑賢|12=draft}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiten|ja|渋川敬典|12=draft}}

By the end of the Edo period, eight families held the position: the {{ill|Shibukawa family|ja|渋川家|12=draft}}, {{ill|Inokai family|ja|猪飼家|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nishikawa family|ja|西川家|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yamaji family|ja|山路家|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yoshida family|ja|吉田家|12=draft}}, {{ill|Okumura family|ja|奥村家|12=draft}}, {{ill|Takahashi family|ja|高橋家|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Adachi family|ja|足立家|12=draft}}. Some families, such as those of {{ill|Nishikawa Joken|ja|西川如見|12=draft}} and {{ill|Takahashi Yoshitoki|ja|高橋至時|12=draft}}, did not maintain their roles until the end of the period. By the late Edo period, only the Shibukawa, Yamaji, and Adachi families continued in the position.
In the 8th year of {{ill|Bunka|ja|文化|12=draft}} (1811), {{ill|Takahashi Kageyasu|ja|高橋景保|12=draft}} proposed establishing the {{ill|Bansho Wage Goyo|ja|蛮書和解御用|12=draft}}, which lasted until the establishment of the {{ill|Institute for Western Studies|ja|蕃書調所|12=draft}} in {{ill|Ansei|ja|安政|12=draft}} 3 (1858). By the late Edo period, the Tenmonkata also handled astronomical observations, surveying, geography, and the translation of Western books, serving as one of the predecessors of {{ill|The University of Tokyo|ja|東京大学|12=draft}}.
== The Tenmonkata Families ==
; Shibukawa Family : Owing to Shibukawa Shunkai's achievements, his family assumed the role of Tenmonkata in Jōkyō 1 (1684) and maintained the position, despite several adoptions, until the late Edo period. : {{ill|Shibukawa Shunkai|ja|渋川春海|12=draft}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mukai|ja|渋川昔尹|12=draft}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiin|ja|渋川敬尹|12=draft}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiya|ja|渋川敬也|12=draft}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Noriyasu|ja|渋川則休|12=draft}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Mitsuhiro|ja|渋川光洪|12=draft}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masakiyo|ja|渋川正清|12=draft}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Masayō|ja|渋川正陽|12=draft}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiyū|ja|渋川景佑|12=draft}} – ({{ill|Shibukawa Keichoku|ja|渋川敬直|12=draft}}) – {{ill|Shibukawa Sukemasa|ja|渋川佑賢|12=draft}} – {{ill|Shibukawa Keiten|ja|渋川敬典|12=draft}}
; Inokai Family : {{ill|Inokai Masakazu|ja|猪飼正一|12=draft}} became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保|12=draft}} 1 (1716). After his death in {{ill|Kanpō|ja|寛保|12=draft}} 1 (1741), the family ceased its role.
; Nishikawa Family : Nishikawa Joken’s son, {{ill|Nishikawa Masayasu|ja|西川正休|12=draft}}, was appointed in {{ill|Enkyō|ja|延享|12=draft}} 4 (1747). The position lasted for two generations. : {{ill|Nishikawa Masayasu|ja|西川正休|12=draft}} – {{ill|Nishikawa Tadataka|ja|西川忠喬|12=draft}}
; Yamaji Family : {{ill|Yamaji Sukezumi|ja|山路主住|12=draft}} became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Meiwa|ja|明和|12=draft}} 1 (1764). His descendants continued until the end of the Edo period. : {{ill|Yamaji Sukezumi|ja|山路主住|12=draft}} – ({{ill|Yamaji Yukiyuki|ja|山路之徽|12=draft}}) – {{ill|Yamaji Tokufū|ja|山路徳風|12=draft}} – {{ill|Yamaji Kanetaka|ja|山路諧孝|12=draft}} – {{ill|Yamaji Akitsune|ja|山路彰常|12=draft}} – ({{ill|Yamaji Akizumi|ja|山路彰善|12=draft}})
; Yoshida Family : {{ill|Sasaki Nagahide|ja|佐々木長秀|12=draft}}, later {{ill|Yoshida Nagahide|ja|吉田秀長|12=draft}}, became Tenmonkata in {{ill|Meiwa|ja|明和|12=draft}} 1 (1764). : {{ill|Yoshida Nagahide|ja|吉田秀長|12=draft}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀升|12=draft}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀賢|12=draft}} – {{ill|Yoshida Hideaki|ja|吉田秀茂|12=draft}}
== Observatories == The first observatory, '''Sitendai''', was established in {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|貞享|12=draft}} 2 (1685) in {{ill|Ushigome Wakamachi|ja|牛込藁町|12=draft}}. It moved to {{ill|Kanda Surugadai|ja|神田駿河台|12=draft}} in {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄|12=draft}} 14 (1701) and later to {{ill|Asakusa|ja|浅草|12=draft}}. In {{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保|12=draft}} 13 (1842), a second observatory was established in {{ill|Kudanzaka|ja|九段坂|12=draft}}. Both were abolished in {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|12=draft}} 2 (1869).
== Abolition of the Tenmonkata == After the {{ill|Taisei Hōkan|ja|大政奉還|12=draft}}, responsibility for calendrical work returned to the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|12=draft}}. In {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|12=draft}} 3 (1870), calendrical duties were transferred to the Ministry of Education's {{ill|Astronomical Bureau|ja|天文暦道局|12=draft}}.
== Related Articles == {{div col}} * {{ill|Inō Tadataka|ja|伊能忠敬|12=draft}} * {{ill|Mitsukuri Shūkichi|ja|箕作秋坪|12=draft}} * {{ill|National Astronomical Observatory of Japan|ja|国立天文台|12=draft}} {{div col end}}
